title: Redesign of the quarterly employment survey | Stats NZ
content:
## Redesign of the quarterly employment survey
09 March 2021, 10:45am
This page describes the changes we've made to the quarterly employment survey (QES). Results of these changes will be reflected in the next QES results to be published in Labour market statistics: March 2021 quarter on 5 May 2021.
- Summary and objectives of the redesign
- Background the the quarterly employment survey
- QES redesign – explanation of changes
- New time-series families

## Summary and objectives of the redesign
- Update and refresh the sample design and methodology to improve the accuracy of published series.
- Simplify the requirements for respondents and thereby reduce respondent burden.
- Introduce a consistent selection methodology and processing system that allows for better comparability with other Stats NZ business data outputs.
- Update processing system design to allow for future changes that can make greater use of administrative data sources from Inland Revenue.

## Background to the quarterly employment survey
The QES covers most types of businesses. The following questions are asked:
- male full-time employees
- male part-time employees
- male ordinary time hours
- male ordinary time pay
- female full-time employees
- female part-time employees
- female ordinary time hours
- female overtime hours
- female ordinary time pay
- female overtime pay.
From this information, the following derived variables are calculated:
- FTEs = (full-time employees) + 0.5 * (part-time employees)
- total pay = ordinary pay + overtime pay
- total hours = ordinary hours + overtime hours
- total filled jobs = (full-time employees) + (part-time employees)
- average weekly paid hours = total hours / FTEs
- average weekly earnings = total pay / FTEs
- average hourly earnings = total pay / total hours.
The key variables obtained by the QES are average weekly earnings, average weekly paid hours, and average hourly earnings. Filled jobs under the new design is defined as the sum of part-time and full-time employees. In the old design it included counts of working proprietors with employees. Average weekly earnings is particularly important as it is used in the setting of Government superannuation payments, benefit increases, and some wage negotiations.
The QES is part of a suite of demand-side employment measures that are released by Stats NZ. User guide for Stats NZ's employment statistics , to be published at the end of March, will summarise these employment measures and the differences between them.

## QES redesign – Explanation of changes
Under the new sample design, the level of data collection has changed from business locations (GEOs) to kind-of-activity units (KAUs).
All units within Stats NZ’s Business Register fit within the structure as shown in figure 1 below. ENT is the enterprise and this is almost always the legal entity, for example a company name like XYZ Ltd, or a government department – such as Stats NZ. Below that is a kind-of-activity unit or KAU. In almost all cases, enterprises have only one KAU. There are a small number of multi-KAUs where a large business may have separate divisions within it. GEOs are physical locations at which the business operates.
Text alternative for Figure 1, Structure of units – Stats NZ. Diagram shows a box at the top with Enterprise (ENT). An arrow below points downwards to a box with Kind-of-activity unit (KAU). Three arrows below this each point to a separate box with Geographic location (GEO).
The redesigned QES will continue to use enterprises as the sampling units but will be collecting information at the KAU level (the old QES was collecting detailed data at the GEO level). This is consistent with most of our quarterly and annual financial surveys, which also collect data at the KAU level. Under the new design, only the number of employees will be collected at the location or GEO level. Compared with the previous design, the new design makes it easier for employers to provide their data. This will reduce response burden and lead to more accurate response data.
Employers will provide data for each KAU by payroll according to how they pay their employees. For example, if they have a weekly payroll run (for example, for employees receiving wages), a fortnightly payroll run (for example, office-based employees on a salary), and then a monthly payroll (for another group of salaried employees), then they might have three payrolls.
Moving to a KAU-based design means we are unable to produce QES data on a regional basis because a KAU can cover employment in multiple regions.
The QES has been operating with an ageing sample design and processing system for several years. There were also aspects of the design and processing system that reduced the flexibility of Stats NZ to respond to changes in the business population over time. Overall, this meant that the quality of the estimates produced by the QES have been degrading, particularly in recent years.
The new design seeks to update the methodology and make it fit-for-purpose. The changes will also enable the design to be updated more easily in the future. This includes the use of more administrative data from Inland Revenue once it becomes available.
A key component of the new design is the Managed Collection approach, which is also used in the quarterly collection of business financial data. Under this approach, large and complex businesses are always included in a survey, with administrative data where possible used for smaller units. At this stage, the QES still requires a targeted sample of small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) as illustrated in figure 2. The Managed Collection approach, is however, standardised and selected on the same basis as the Stats NZ quarterly business financial collection. This will allow greater comparability and coherence across outputs.
Text alternative for figure 2, New collection model for the quarterly employment survey. Diagram shows a rectangular box split into two sections. The smaller top section contains the word 'large and complex businesses (managed collection)'. The larger bottom section has within it an oval labelled 'small and medium enterprises (targeted sample)'.
The targeted sample design is similar in form to the old sample design. However, there have been updates to the stratification boundaries reflecting industry changes since the last redesign. Stratification is done to a common industry-level relative standard error (RSE) for design variables. Full-coverage Managed Collection units are incorporated. The statistical unit is KAU rather than GEO – again this will provide greater comparability with other Stats NZ business data outputs, such as the quarterly financial collection and the annual enterprise survey, which are both designed at KAU level.
The sample has been significantly refreshed to reflect changes in the underlying population. About half of the sampled enterprises in the new design were in the old sample, with the balance being new to the survey.
Another major improvement to the design is that data for state sector schools is now full coverage rather than being a small selection of individual schools under a stratified sample. A feed of payroll information from the Ministry of Education is used to provide this portion of the QES.
The aforementioned changes to the sample is a significant improvement, and will lead to more accurate estimation of all variables. Not surprisingly, this has led to a level shift in the outputs data. Graphs showing the nature of this change to both filled jobs, and average weekly earnings are shown below in the section on backcasting. As the new sample design allows the units to move strata and/or industries every quarter, the result will be a more a variable series from quarter to quarter than has been the case with the previous design.
The level shifts seen are a combination of the following factors
- Quality improvement:
- units will now change strata as they grow out of them and be appropriately weighted for their size
- selection into the survey will be based on more up-to-date information about the Australian and New Zealand Standard Industrial Classification (ANZSIC) of the business and employee count
- several industries have reduced standard errors compared with the previous design
- full coverage for all large and/or complex businesses with the Managed Collection, which has more extensive full coverage than the previous design
- improved coverage of all state sector schools data. Historically Stats NZ only included units that were selected in the sample.
- the statistical unit will be KAU rather than GEO, meaning that sample weights will more accurately represent the random sampling process
- industry estimates will be based on KAU ANZSIC division rather than GEO
- the population is at least 10 percent larger than the previous QES population and includes some large Managed Collection units that were previously excluded from QES.
All-time series, apart from those related to regions and those related to working proprietors, will continue to be produced. Details of the new time series families and identifiers are discussed in time-series families .
The publication date will remain the same, with data being released as part of Stats NZ’s quarterly labour market statistics release.
As noted in the section on sample design, a standard sample redesign has not been done for QES for a longer than usual time. This resulted in the sample becoming less representative over time. To ensure a consistent time series, it is important that at the same time as the sample changes, backcasting also be done. Backcasting creates new back series for each series so that they are methodologically and structurally consistent with the refreshed design.
The agreed solution was to backcast at unit record level as much as possible, with data prior to September 2009 calculated using an aggregate ratio method.

#### Data from September 2009 to current
Each unit is placed in the historic series into the strata that it belongs to in each quarter.  Weights are calculated based on the units active in the industry at that time, so units can change strata over time, with ceased units not being counted in weight calculations. The industry a business is selected into was determined by its historic industry (which can change over time, and is based on more updated snapshots from the Stats NZ Business Register), and its strata was based on the employee count (EC) for that unit at each quarter. The strata boundaries used were the historic ones used from 2009 onwards, not the newly calculated boundaries from the redesign.
Once this was done weights could be recalculated for each selection cell as the count of population businesses in the strata divided by the sample size of businesses in each stratum. These weights were then applied to get industry totals for all published variables. This method was used back to the September 2009 quarter, which was the last redesign of the QES. The 2009 redesign supported the transition to ANZSIC06 from ANZSIC96.

#### Data before September 2009
For quarters before 2009 an aggregate ratio method was applied. This is an aggregate method, and is not applied at the unit level, and so may not be as accurate as re-assigning units and re-calculating weights. It will preserve the quarterly movements in the previously published figures. Ratios were calculated for each of the questionnaire variables, for three aggregated levels: industry, size of firm, and sector. The ratios were calculated using the four closest quarters between the revised series and the original series. The factor for this method is the ratio of the average of the earliest four quarters of backcast data to the most recent actual data.
Once back casting was completed, each series was assessed for quality. The most recent data was used to validate the back series. As we collect further data, particularly under the new design for the March 2021 quarter, we may need to make further adjustments to the back series.

#### Impacts of backcasting
Total filled jobs (TFJ) and average weekly earnings (AWE) are illustrated below in figures 3 and 4, where the currently published QES series and the backcasted values under the new design are compared.  Filled jobs shows the greatest level of change with an upwards level shift under the new design. Average weekly earnings are slightly higher under the new design with the difference growing over time as the sample degraded.
Total filled jobs, currently published and backcast series,  September 2009 quarter to December 2020 quarter
Average weekly earnings, currently published and backcast series, September 2009 quarter to December 2020 quarter

## New time-series families
We have created new families and time-series identifiers for the data produced using our new design. The old families will no longer be updated and will be labelled as discontinued on Infoshare. The old series will still be accessible by selecting the ‘show discontinued’ option from Infoshare.
The Infoshare family for the new data is:
Earnings and Employment Survey (QES) – QEM, within the Subject Category ‘Work income and spending’
The new time series identifiers differ from the old ones by just one character – an ‘M’ has replaced the ‘X’ in the prefix, so in all cases ‘QEX’ has been replaced by ‘QEM’.
At this stage we are releasing the backcast series to September 2009. The remaining backcast series is still being finalised and will be released at a later date. Seasonally adjusted series are also still being finalised and will also be released at a later date.
As noted above in the sample design section, the eventual goal is to be able to incorporate more administrative data into the QES, thereby reducing the need to survey small and medium-sized businesses. Inland Revenue has recently begun to obtain paid hours data from employers. Hours paid is a critical component of the current QES, and at present a large part of why Stats NZ still runs a sample survey. The quality and completeness of the hours data being collected by Inland Revenue has yet to be assessed, but Stats NZ will be investigating this as we seek to try to move towards a collection model for the QES, which would look like the diagram in figure 5 below.
Text alternative for figure 5, Future collection model for the quarterly employment survey. Diagram shows a rectangular box split into two sections. The smaller top section contains the wording 'large and complex businesses (managed collection)'. The larger bottom section has within it the wording 'small and medium enterprises payday filing data transformed (once hours paid available)'.
